However, most existing approaches overlook fine-grained semantic alignment, which can constrain the quality of the generated representations. To tackle this problem, we propose the Multi-Grained Vision-and-Language Alignment (MGVLA) model, which effectively leverages multi-grained correspondences between medical images and texts at different levels, including disease, instance, and token levels. For disease-level alignment, our approach adopts the concept of contrastive learning and uses medical terminologies detected from textual reports as soft labels to guide the alignment process. At the instance level, we propose a strategy for sampling hard negatives, where images and texts with the same disease type but differing in details such as disease locations and severity are considered as hard negatives. This strategy helps our approach to better distinguish between positive and negative image-text pairs, ultimately enhancing the quality of our learned representations. For token-level alignment, we employ a masking and recovery technique to achieve fine-grained semantic alignment between patches and sub-words. This approach effectively aligns the different levels of granularity between the image and language modalities. To assess the efficacy of our MGVLA model, we conduct comprehensive experiments on the image-text retrieval and phrase grounding tasks.

4. Text-Image Co-Alignment for Weakly Supervised Polyp Segmentation. (IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging 2026, cites: 0)
   Fully supervised polyp segmentation relies on costly pixel-level annotations. Although semi- and weakly supervised methods reduce annotation requirements, they still depend on partial mask supervision. Text-supervised segmentation is a promising alternative; however, for polyps, the key challenge is to ground instance-specific phrases to the correct lesion region under cluttered backgrounds and large appearance variations. Existing approaches often rely on coarse text-image alignment, limiting precise region-level semantic correspondence. In this paper, we propose Text-Image Co-Alignment (TICoA), a text-supervised framework for polyp segmentation. TICoA leverages large language models (LLMs)-generated structured clinical descriptions as weak supervision and formulates segmentation as a fine-grained phrase-region coalignment problem. Through contrastive learning, TICoA explicitly associates query phrases with corresponding image regions to achieve robust semantic grounding under weak supervision. Architecturally, we adopt a State-Space Model (Mamba) to efficiently model long-range dependencies with linear computational complexity. To support effective cross-modal interaction, we further design a dedicated Mamba Fusion module with a Bi-Dimension Fusion (BiDF) strategy, which progressively propagates information along spatial and channel dimensions. Experiments on polyp datasets, with additional validation on skin lesion segmentation, demonstrate that TICoA is competitive with state-of-the-art weakly supervised methods. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/silentyuchen/TICoA.

5. Revisiting Multimodal Representation in Contrastive Learning: From Patch and Token Embeddings to Finite Discrete Tokens (Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2023, cites: 57)
   Contrastive learning-based vision-language pretraining approaches, such as CLIP, have demonstrated great success in many vision-language tasks. These methods achieve cross-modal alignment by encoding a matched image-text pair with similar feature embeddings, which are generated by aggregating information from visual patches and language tokens. However, direct aligning cross-modal information using such representations is challenging, as visual patches and text tokens differ in semantic levels and granularities. To alleviate this issue, we propose a Finite Discrete Tokens (FDT) based multimodal representation. FDT is a set of learnable tokens representing certain visualsemantic concepts. Both images and texts are embedded using shared FDT by first grounding multimodal inputs to FDT space and then aggregating the activated FDT representations. The matched visual and semantic concepts are enforced to be represented by the same set of discrete tokens by a sparse activation constraint. As a result, the granularity gap between the two modalities is reduced. Through both quantitative and qualitative analyses, we demonstrate that using FDT representations in CLIP-style models improves cross-modal alignment and performance in visual recognition and vision-language downstream tasks. Furthermore, we show that our method can learn more comprehensive representations, and the learned FDT capture meaningful cross-modal correspondence, ranging from objects to actions and attributes.11The source code can be found at https://github.com/yuxiaochen1103/FDT.

6. AlignCAT: Visual-Linguistic Alignment of Category and Attribute for Weakly Supervised Visual Grounding (ACM Multimedia 2025, cites: 1)
   Weakly supervised visual grounding (VG) aims to locate objects in images based on text descriptions. Despite significant progress, existing methods lack strong cross-modal reasoning to distinguish subtle semantic differences in text expressions due to category-based and attribute-based ambiguity. To address these challenges, we introduce AlignCAT, a novel query-based semantic matching framework for weakly supervised VG. To enhance visual-linguistic alignment, we propose a coarse-grained alignment module that utilizes category information and global context, effectively mitigating interference from category-inconsistent objects. Subsequently, a fine-grained alignment module leverages descriptive information and captures word-level text features to achieve attribute consistency. By exploiting linguistic cues to their fullest extent, our proposed AlignCAT progressively filters out misaligned visual queries and enhances contrastive learning efficiency. Extensive experiments on three VG benchmarks, namely RefCOCO, RefCOCO+, and RefCOCOg, verify the superiority of AlignCAT against existing weakly supervised methods on two VG tasks. Our code is available at: https://github.com/I2-Multimedia-Lab/AlignCAT.

7. Claim-Level Clinical Fact-Checking of Chest X-Ray Reports via Assertion-Aware Contrastive Vision–Language Learning (2025 International Conference on Intelligent Computing, Information and Control Systems (ICOIICS) 2025, cites: 0)
   To check factual consistency in chest X-ray (CXR) reports, RAD-CHECK was built as a vision-language system. Standard systems often treat factuality like a simple yes/no choice, or else rely only on text checks. This leaves gaps for real clinical work. RAD-CHECK instead looks at each finding at the claim level, deciding whether the evidence is entailed, contradicted, or perhaps just neutral compared to the linked image. Two important innovations are built in. First, contrastive vision-language pretraining lines up image regions with written findings. Second, an assertion filter with UMLS mapping spots negations and uncertainties in the text. Training used paired CXR images from both MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert, with “hard negative” cases added by mismatching or slightly corrupting claims. This approach increased discrimination. At prediction time the model uses a three-way NLI classifier. For interpretability it provides token-level saliency for the text side, and Grad-CAM for the image side. These explanations, while not perfect, give users some clues about why the model reached a decision. Test results show promising numbers: AUROC about 0.91, AP near 0.90, and precision of about 0.85 when recall is 0.90. Importantly, sensitivity stayed high at clinically relevant thresholds, and the alert burden was modest enough for use in practice. Calibration steps further improved stability, though performance may vary with different patient groups. Overall, the findings indicate that claim-level multimodal inference especially when joined with assertion filtering and visual-textual alignment improves accuracy and dependability of radiology report checks. The system is not flawless, but it offers a transparent and more practical route toward automated quality control in clinical imaging.

8. Contrastive Vision-Language Alignment Makes Efficient Instruction Learner (arXiv.org 2023, cites: 5)
   We study the task of extending the large language model (LLM) into a vision-language instruction-following model. This task is crucial but challenging since the LLM is trained on text modality only, making it hard to effectively digest the visual modality. To address this, existing methods typically train a visual adapter to align the representation between a pre-trained vision transformer (ViT) and the LLM by a generative image captioning loss. However, we find that the generative objective can only produce weak alignment for vision and language, making the aligned vision-language model very hungry for the instruction fine-tuning data. In this paper, we propose CG-VLM that applies both Contrastive and Generative alignment objectives to effectively align the representation of ViT and LLM. Different from image level and sentence level alignment in common contrastive learning settings, CG-VLM aligns the image-patch level features and text-token level embeddings, which, however, is very hard to achieve as no explicit grounding patch-token relation provided in standard image captioning datasets. To address this issue, we propose to maximize the averaged similarity between pooled image-patch features and text-token embeddings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed CG-VLM produces strong vision-language alignment and is an efficient instruction learner. For example, using only 10% instruction tuning data, we reach 95% performance of state-of-the-art method LLaVA [29] on the zero-shot ScienceQA-Image benchmark.

9. Collaborative Text-to-Image Generation via Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning and Semantic Fusion (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Multimodal text-to-image generation remains constrained by the difficulty of maintaining semantic alignment and professional-level detail across diverse visual domains. We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that coordinates domain-specialized agents (e.g., focused on architecture, portraiture, and landscape imagery) within two coupled subsystems: a text enhancement module and an image generation module, each augmented with multimodal integration components. Agents are trained using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) under a composite reward function that balances semantic similarity, linguistic visual quality, and content diversity. Cross-modal alignment is enforced through contrastive learning, bidirectional attention, and iterative feedback between text and image. Across six experimental settings, our system significantly enriches generated content (word count increased by 1614%) while reducing ROUGE-1 scores by 69.7%. Among fusion methods, Transformer-based strategies achieve the highest composite score (0.521), despite occasional stability issues. Multimodal ensembles yield moderate consistency (ranging from 0.444 to 0.481), reflecting the persistent challenges of cross-modal semantic grounding. These findings underscore the promise of collaborative, specialization-driven architectures for advancing reliable multimodal generative systems.

10. Anatomy-Aware Conditional Image-Text Retrieval (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 0)
   Image-Text Retrieval (ITR) finds broad applications in healthcare, aiding clinicians and radiologists by automatically retrieving relevant patient cases in the database given the query image and/or report, for more efficient clinical diagnosis and treatment, especially for rare diseases. However conventional ITR systems typically only rely on global image or text representations for measuring patient image/report similarities, which overlook local distinctiveness across patient cases. This often results in suboptimal retrieval performance. In this paper, we propose an Anatomical Location-Conditioned Image-Text Retrieval (ALC-ITR) framework, which, given a query image and the associated suspicious anatomical region(s), aims to retrieve similar patient cases exhibiting the same disease or symptoms in the same anatomical region. To perform location-conditioned multimodal retrieval, we learn a medical Relevance-Region-Aligned Vision Language (RRA-VL) model with semantic global-level and region-/word-level alignment to produce generalizable, well-aligned multi-modal representations. Additionally, we perform location-conditioned contrastive learning to further utilize cross-pair region-level contrastiveness for improved multi-modal retrieval. We show that our proposed RRA-VL achieves state-of-the-art localization performance in phase-grounding tasks, and satisfying multi-modal retrieval performance with or without location conditioning. Finally, we thoroughly investigate the generalizability and explainability of our proposed ALC-ITR system in providing explanations and preliminary diagnosis reports given retrieved patient cases (conditioned on anatomical regions), with proper off-the-shelf LLM prompts.

1. CAMVR: Context-Adaptive Multi-View Representation Learning for Dense Retrieval (IEEE International Joint Conference on Neural Network 2023, cites: 1)
   The recently proposed MVR (Multi-View Repre-sentation) model achieves remarkable performance for open-domain dense retrieval. In MVR, matching multi-view queries is enabled by encoding the document into multiple representations. However, these representations tend to collapse into the same one when the percentage of documents answering multiple queries in the training data is low. Here, we propose a Context-Adaptive Multi-View Representation (CAMVR) learning framework that explicitly avoids this collapse problem by aligning each viewer token with different document snippets. In CAMVR, each viewer token is placed before a snippet to capture the local and global information with consideration that answers of different view queries may scatter in one document. The view of the snippet containing the answer is also used to explicitly supervise the learning process, from which the interpretability of the view representation is provided. 1. Text-driven Multiplanar Visual Interaction for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation (International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer-Assisted Intervention 2025, cites: 3)
   Semi-supervised medical image segmentation is a crucial technique for alleviating the high cost of data annotation. When labeled data is limited, textual information can provide additional context to enhance visual semantic understanding. However, research exploring the use of textual data to enhance visual semantic embeddings in 3D medical imaging tasks remains scarce. In this paper, we propose a novel text-driven multiplanar visual interaction framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation (termed Text-SemiSeg), which consists of three main modules: Text-enhanced Multiplanar Representation (TMR), Category-aware Semantic Alignment (CSA), and Dynamic Cognitive Augmentation (DCA). Specifically, TMR facilitates text-visual interaction through planar mapping, thereby enhancing the category awareness of visual features. CSA performs cross-modal semantic alignment between the text features with introduced learnable variables and the intermediate layer of visual features. DCA reduces the distribution discrepancy between labeled and unlabeled data through their interaction, thus improving the model's robustness. Finally, experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that our model effectively enhances visual features with textual information and outperforms other methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/taozh2017/Text-SemiSeg.

2. Pre-training Cross-Modal Retrieval by Expansive Lexicon-Patch Alignment (International Conference on Language Resources and Evaluation 2024, cites: 1)

Nonetheless, besides semantic matching learned by contrastive learning, cross-modal retrieval also largely relies on object matching between modalities. This necessitates fine-grained categorical discriminative learning, which however suffers from scarce data in full-supervised scenarios and information asymmetry in weakly-supervised scenarios when applied to cross-modal retrieval. To address these issues, we propose expansive lexicon-patch alignment (ELA) to align image patches with a vocabulary rather than only the words explicitly in the text for annotation-free alignment and information augmentation, thus enabling more effective fine-grained categorical discriminative learning for cross-modal retrieval. Experimental results show that ELA could effectively learn representative fine-grained information and outperform state-of-the-art methods on cross-modal retrieval.

3. MoralCLIP: Contrastive Alignment of Vision-and-Language Representations with Moral Foundations Theory (ACM Multimedia 2025, cites: 0)
   Recent advances in vision-language models have enabled rich semantic understanding across modalities. However, these encoding methods lack the ability to interpret or reason about the moral dimensions of content---a crucial aspect of human cognition. In this paper, we address this gap by introducing MoralCLIP, a novel embedding representation method that extends multimodal learning with explicit moral grounding based on Moral Foundations Theory (MFT). Our approach integrates visual and textual moral cues into a unified embedding space, enabling cross-modal moral alignment. MoralCLIP is grounded on the multi-label dataset Social-Moral Image Database to identify co-occurring moral foundations in visual content. For MoralCLIP training, we design a moral data augmentation strategy to scale our annotated dataset to 15,000 image-text pairs labeled with MFT-aligned dimensions. Our results demonstrate that explicit moral supervision improves both unimodal and multimodal understanding of moral content, establishing a foundation for morally-aware AI systems capable of recognizing and aligning with human moral values.

4. Adaptive Pseudo Text Augmentation for Noise-Robust Text-to-Image Person Re-Identification (Italian National Conference on Sensors 2025, cites: 1)
   Highlights What are the main findings? The proposed Adaptive Pseudo Text Augmentation framework effectively identifies noisy image–text correspondence in text-to-image person re-identification using a Gaussian mixture model and recovers valuable data through pseudo-text generation with a multimodal large language model. The model achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance on CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES, and RSTPReid datasets, demonstrating superior robustness under noisy supervision. What are the implications of the main findings? The proposed method improves the reliability of cross-modal alignment in real-world T2I-ReID datasets that contain coarse or mismatched text descriptions. It provides a general and extensible strategy for noise-resilient learning in other multimodal tasks involving imperfect annotations. Abstract Text-to-image person re-identification (T2I-ReID) aims to retrieve pedestrians from images/videos based on textual descriptions. However, most methods implicitly assume that training image–text pairs are correctly aligned, while in practice, issues such as under-correlated and falsely correlated image–text pairs arise due to coarse-grained text annotations and erroneous textual descriptions. To address this problem, we propose a T2I-ReID method based on noise identification and pseudo-text generation. We first extracts image–text features using the Contrastive Language–Image Pre-Training model (CLIP), then employs the token fusion model to select and fuse informative local token features, resulting in token fusion embedding (TFE) for fine-grained representations. To identify noisy image–text pairs, we apply the two-component Gaussian mixture model (GMM) to fit the per-sample loss distributions computed by the predictions of basic feature embedding (BFE) and TFE. Finally, when the noise identification tends to stabilize, we employ a multimodal large language model (MLLM) to generate pseudo-texts that replace the noisy text, facilitating learning more reliable visual–semantic associations and cross-modal alignment under noisy conditions. Extensive experiments on the CUHK-PEDES, ICFG-PEDES, and RSTPReid datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed model and the good compatibility with other baselines.

5. VLMixer: Unpaired Vision-Language Pre-training via Cross-Modal CutMix (International Conference on Machine Learning 2022, cites: 59)
   Existing vision-language pre-training (VLP) methods primarily rely on paired image-text datasets, which are either annotated by enormous human labors, or crawled from the internet followed by elaborate data cleaning techniques. To reduce the dependency on well-aligned image-text pairs, it is promising to directly leverage the large-scale text-only and image-only corpora. This paper proposes a data augmentation method, namely cross-modal CutMix (CMC), for implicit cross-modal alignment learning in unpaired VLP. Specifically, CMC transforms natural sentences from the textual view into a multi-modal view, where visually-grounded words in a sentence are randomly replaced by diverse image patches with similar semantics. There are several appealing proprieties of the proposed CMC. First, it enhances the data diversity while keeping the semantic meaning intact for tackling problems where the aligned data are scarce; Second, by attaching cross-modal noise on uni-modal data, it guides models to learn token-level interactions across modalities for better denoising. Furthermore, we present a new unpaired VLP method, dubbed as VLMixer, that integrates CMC with contrastive learning to pull together the uni-modal and multi-modal views for better instance-level alignments among different modalities. Extensive experiments on five downstream tasks show that VLMixer could surpass previous state-of-the-art unpaired VLP methods.

6. Ultrasound Vision-Language Alignment via Contrastive Learning ( 2026, cites: 0)
   Ultrasound foundation models have achieved strong performance on structured prediction tasks but remain exclusively vision-based, limiting zero-shot and few-shot transfer to novel tasks where task-specific annotation is scarce. We address this gap with EchoCare-CLIP, a CLIP-style dual-encoder contrastive framework that aligns ultrasound images with clinical text in a shared embedding space. We curate a multi-organ corpus of over 16K image-text pairs spanning breast, liver, lung, and thyroid, with over 78% of captions derived from expert-annotated reports, and complement the remainder with a three-tier template-based and LLM-based caption generation pipeline. We evaluate model configurations spanning two text encoder families (CLIP, BioClinicalBERT) and two caption strategies (template-based, LLM-generated) against OpenAI CLIP and BiomedCLIP baselines. Our trained models consistently improve cross-modal alignment over baselines, with the best configuration achieving a paired alignment score of 0.682. However, stronger alignment does not guarantee better downstream performance: CLIP-based variants with partial fine-tuning achieve the strongest zero-shot classification on external held-out datasets (0.709 on BUSI; 0.626 on AULI), while full end-to-end fine-tuning degrades transfer due to overfitting. On linear probing and few-shot adaptation, model rankings are dataset-dependent, reflecting a trade-off between domain adaptation and representational generalizability. We further show that template-based captions match or outperform LLM-generated captions, suggesting lexical diversity is not a proxy for caption quality. Taken together, our results demonstrate that ultrasound vision-language alignment is achievable from public data alone, but robust clinical transfer requires careful balancing of domain adaptation, encoder capacity, and caption supervision quality.

7. Enhancing Pathology Vision-Language Pre-Training: A Robust Baseline (IEEE International Conference on Bioinformatics and Biomedicine 2025, cites: 0)
   Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP) has demonstrated remarkable performance in aligning image and text representations across various domains, including medical imaging. However, CLIP relies heavily on large-scale paired datasets, which are scarce in the medical field, particularly in pathology imaging. Existing methods rely on web-scraped datasets that often contain noisy and irrelevant information, leading to poor generalization across different datasets, magnifications, and clinical settings due to incomplete or low-quality text. To address these limitations, we propose a hierarchical self-supervised vision-language framework that combines CLIP with Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), called PIVLM, to enhance feature representation for pathology images. Our framework introduces multi-level alignment at the instance, patch-token, region, and prototype levels, enabling the model to capture finegrained semantic correspondences between images and texts. By leveraging image augmentation for SSL, our method reduces reliance on noisy and incomplete textual information, ensuring robust feature learning. Experiments on five datasets for pathology images show that PIVLM achieves state-of-theart performance on zero-shot tasks compared to its CLIP-like counterparts. These results present a robust baseline for future research in medical image analysis, particularly in pathology, and opens new avenues for addressing the challenges of cross-modal alignment in medical imaging. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/PIVLM-F9B1/.

8. Cattle-CLIP: A Multimodal Framework for Cattle Behaviour Recognition (arXiv.org 2025, cites: 2)
   Robust behaviour recognition in real-world farm environments remains challenging due to several data-related limitations, including the scarcity of well-annotated livestock video datasets and the substantial domain gap between large-scale pre-training corpora and agricultural surveillance footage. To address these challenges, we propose Cattle-CLIP, a domain-adaptive vision-language framework that reformulates cattle behaviour recognition as cross-modal semantic alignment rather than purely visual classification. Instead of directly fine-tuning visual backbones, Cattle-CLIP incorporates a temporal integration module to extend image-level contrastive pre-training to video-based behaviour understanding, enabling consistent semantic alignment across time. To mitigate the distribution shift between web-scale image-text data used for the pre-trained model and real-world cattle surveillance footage, we further introduce tailored augmentation strategies and specialised behaviour prompts. Furthermore, we construct CattleBehaviours6, a curated and behaviour-consistent video dataset comprising 1905 annotated clips across six indoor behaviours to support model training and evaluation. Beyond serving as a benchmark for our proposed method, the dataset provides a standardised ethogram definition, offering a practical resource for future research in livestock behaviour analysis. Cattle-CLIP is evaluated under both fully-supervised and few-shot learning scenarios, with a particular focus on data-scarce behaviour recognition, an important yet under-explored goal in livestock monitoring. Experiments show that Cattle-CLIP achieves 96.1% overall accuracy across six behaviours in supervised settings, with near-perfect recall for feeding, drinking and standing-ruminating behaviours, and demonstrates robust generalisation with limited data in few-shot scenarios.

Reasoning: The proposed UniX-CL combines five distinct mechanisms: (1) teacher-guided listwise/RankNet distillation from a frozen VLM over unpaired corpora for cross-modal supervision, (2) multi-view viewer-token embeddings with max-aggregation (from MVR/CAMVR in IR), (3) SimCSE + semantic-controlled counterfactual text augmentation, (4) factual-counterfactual saliency-driven visual masking for grounding, and (5) a copula-based distributional alignment regularizer. Searches surfaced: CM2 (copula cross-modal alignment, seed #5), VLMixer (unpaired VLP via cross-modal CutMix + contrastive), MVR/CAMVR (viewer tokens for dense retrieval), FIRST (RankNet listwise distillation from reranker orderings for retrieval), and "Counterfactual Contrastive Learning for Weakly-Supervised Vision-Language Grounding." No single paper combines all five mechanisms, and critically, the specific combination of frozen-teacher listwise ranking distillation over large unpaired corpora + copula regularizer + factual/counterfactual saliency masking for cross-modal alignment has not been surfaced. Individual components exist in prior work but their joint application to the semi-supervised cross-modal alignment problem is novel. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this problem, with individual mechanisms found separately.

Prior works:
  - Cross-Modal Alignment via Variational Copula Modelling / CM2 (2024): Uses parametric copula to model cross-modal joint distribution for multimodal alignment — directly overlaps with the copula regularizer component.
  - VLMixer: Unpaired Vision-Language Pre-training via Cross-Modal CutMix (2022): Addresses unpaired VLP with contrastive learning — overlaps with the semi-supervised unpaired cross-modal learning problem framing.
  - Multi-View Document Representation Learning for Open-Domain Dense Retrieval / MVR (2022): Introduces viewer tokens with max-pooling for multi-view document embeddings — directly overlaps with the multi-view image embedding component.
  - FIRST: Faster Improved Listwise Reranking with Single Token Decoding (2024): Uses RankNet listwise loss over reranker orderings for retrieval distillation — overlaps with the listwise ranking distillation objective.
  - Counterfactual Contrastive Learning for Weakly-Supervised Vision-Language Grounding (2020): Uses counterfactual contrastive objectives for vision-language grounding — overlaps with the factual/counterfactual contrastive component.

You synthesize a method that addresses the user's query by drawing on
a set of retrieved research seeds. Each seed is a published
contribution with a (problem, method, domain, paper_title) tuple, and
it arrives with a "Retrieved via" lens — the text (raw query or a
paraphrase reframing the query for a specific domain) that surfaced
this seed during retrieval. Use the lens as a hint about WHY this
seed was considered; the paraphrase lens often reveals an analogy
between the query and a mechanism family the raw query alone would
not have surfaced. The seeds may come from any subset of ML domains;
use those whose mechanisms genuinely help, and do not force
connections to seeds that do not fit.

Distractor handling: when a seed's lens explicitly indicates it was
NOT retrieved for this query (e.g., "uniform random sample of the
library; no targeted retrieval"), treat that seed as a distractor.
Ignore it unless its mechanism genuinely fits the query for reasons
unrelated to retrieval. It is correct and expected to leave such
seeds out of inspired_by; do not pad inspired_by to make it look
like the distractors were used.

Your output MUST be JSON:
  {
    "method":    "<3–5 paragraphs describing the proposed method end-to-end>",
    "rationale": "<why this addresses the user query, in 1–3 paragraphs>",
    "inspired_by": [
      { "seed_id": "<from one of the provided seeds>",
        "domain":  "<that seed's domain>",
        "borrowed_aspect": "<short noun phrase: what was taken from this seed>" },
      ...
    ]
  }

Provenance: list a seed in inspired_by when its mechanism genuinely
contributed to the method. You MAY include steps that no seed
supports when they are necessary glue logic, problem-specific
adaptation, or a novel addition; do not omit a needed step just
because no seed inspired it. Do not pad inspired_by with seeds that
did not actually contribute.

## Query
Aligned text-image pairs are scarce in some domains while
unimodal data is abundant; how can we learn cross-modal
representations via contrastive objectives that exploit the
abundant unimodal data?


## Retrieved seeds

### Seed 2112b9511767 [IR_REC] — "GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
- Method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

### Seed a9647aa8fe5e [IR_REC] — "Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition"
- Retrieved via: (uniform random sample of the library; no targeted retrieval)
- Problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
- Method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

### Seed 3917da5fd88a [LLM_NLP] — "Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement"

You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Aligned text-image pairs are scarce in some domains while
unimodal data is abundant; how can we learn cross-modal
representations via contrastive objectives that exploit the
abundant unimodal data?


### Proposed method
We propose UniX-Align, a semi-supervised contrastive framework that learns cross-modal image–text representations by leveraging abundant unimodal corpora and a small set of aligned pairs. Two modality-specific encoders are first pretrained with strong unimodal self-supervision (e.g., image contrastive learning and sentence-level contrastive learning) and then connected via a shared semantic prototype bank. Each encoder projects samples to a common embedding and outputs a soft assignment over K shared prototypes; the modality-specific projection heads remain distinct, while the prototype bank is shared to anchor a joint semantic space. A lightweight confidence head attached to the prototype layer provides per-sample uncertainty used to weight noisy supervision signals.

To turn unimodal data into useful cross-modal supervision, we mine pseudo pairs with two complementary channels. First, we build a cross-modal pivot space using visual taggers for images and keyword/entity extraction for text, and connect samples with high tag-overlap; a general-domain teacher (e.g., CLIP) provides soft similarity scores as additional weak signals, but these are down-weighted by the confidence head and an uncertainty-guided EMA schedule so that only stable pseudo links accumulate influence over time. Second, we construct set-level training tuples by sampling small groups of images and texts that share coarse tags. We then apply a permutation-invariant, set-to-set contrastive objective that encourages correct within-set assignments to score higher than mismatched ones, together with an auxiliary “mix-and-separate” prediction that asks the model to recover which text in the set is most responsible for each image (and vice versa). This disambiguates associations without requiring exact instance-level labels upfront.

Training follows a coarse-to-fine curriculum. We begin by aligning prototype distributions at the cluster level: separately cluster unimodal embeddings, link clusters via tag overlap and teacher scores, and minimize cross-modal divergence between their prototype assignments while preserving high-entropy, diverse usage of prototypes. As pseudo pairs stabilize, we transition to instance-level contrastive training on high-confidence matches, interleaving: (a) supervised InfoNCE on the scarce true pairs, (b) confidence-weighted InfoNCE on pseudo pairs, (c) the set-to-set mix-and-separate loss, and (d) a structure-preserving term that encourages local neighborhood consistency across modalities so that unimodal proximity is reflected cross-modally. A memory bank maintains embeddings and tag metadata to refresh pseudo neighbors periodically; pseudo-pair weights are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA so noisy links decay while consistent links grow.

At convergence, both encoders and the shared prototype bank define an aligned space suitable for retrieval and transfer. Inference uses standard cosine similarity between image and text embeddings, with optional prototype-temperature calibration learned during training to improve zero-shot retrieval in the low-pair regime.

### Rationale
The core challenge is to exploit plentiful unimodal data without reliable alignment. UniX-Align converts unimodal corpora into usable cross-modal signals in two ways: (1) prototype-anchored alignment that ties both modalities to a common semantic scaffold learned at scale from unimodal pretraining, and (2) pseudo supervision that is robust to noise through set-level mix-and-separate objectives, confidence weighting, and an uncertainty-guided EMA curriculum. This lets the model learn which image features go with which textual concepts even when exact pairings are sparse.

Set-to-set training leverages weak co-occurrence and topical similarity to create contrastive pressure without requiring instance-level pairs, while the confidence-weighted EMA and prototype-based uncertainty mitigate confirmation bias from imperfect teachers and noisy taggers. The curriculum from cluster-level to instance-level alignment further reduces brittleness: the model first learns broad cross-modal semantics before committing to fine-grained matches, improving stability and final retrieval performance under scarce paired supervision.

1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

   method:  Propose a Visual Prompt Learning Strategy with Uncertainty-Guided Updating (VPLU): learn pixel-level visual prompts added to input images (used in both cloud training and on-device inference) to pull the device test distribution closer to cloud training. Prompts are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA where the update weight increases with sample uncertainty, and are combined with adversarial feature alignment on the teacher (domain discriminator + GRL) and pseudo-label knowledge transfer to the student (high-confidence filtering). Only prompts (~0.4% of model parameters) and the lightweight student are sent to devices, yielding consistent gains (e.g., +3.3% over pseudo-label baseline; +2.7% from U-EMA) and alleviating forgetting across continual domains.

9. [ROBOTICS] TwinTac: A Wide-Range, Highly Sensitive Tactile Sensor with Real-to-Sim Digital Twin Sensor Model (2509.10063)
   problem: Non-optical tactile sensors often trade off sensitivity for range (or vice versa), limiting their usefulness in manipulation that spans delicate touch to high-force contacts.
   method:  PhysTac integrates eight MEMS pressure taxels (CPS135B-1500D) beneath an Ecoflex 00-31 elastomer, multiplexed over I2C (55 Hz) to achieve both ultra-high sensitivity and a wide pressure range. The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

10. [RL] Inductive-bias-driven Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Schedules in Heterogeneous Clusters (1909.02119)
   problem: Adding a Bayesian model to guide RL typically requires full probabilistic inference during training, which is computationally prohibitive for periodic retraining in real systems.
   method:  The paper introduces a sampling-based gradient estimator for Bayesian networks that computes gradients of BN parameters without performing full posterior inference. By generating samples and expanding only the necessary BN subgraph, it recursively estimates gradients with favorable complexity (especially on polytree BNs), decoupling BN training from the inference procedure used to form the belief state. This accelerates learning, yielding about 4× faster training than belief-propagation-based full inference and ~2.2× better sample efficiency than a model-free RNN baseline.

11. [MULTIMODAL] Progress-Aware Video Frame Captioning (2412.02071)
   problem: Downstream video understanding and selection tasks lack temporally fine-grained supervision to pick informative frames and reason about ordered events.
   method:  Use ProgressCaptioner’s per-frame, progress-aware captions as an intermediate textual representation. An LLM compares consecutive captions to select keyframes that mark action progression, improving action recognition when replacing uniformly sampled frames. The same captions guide zero-shot frame-wise classification (via LLM multi-choice over labels) and enhance zero-shot video QA on NExT-QA (ATP-Hard), surpassing prior zero-shot baselines and improving temporal reasoning performance.

12. [ROBOTICS] Textile Taxonomy and Classification Using Pulling and Twisting (2103.09555)
   problem: Robotics lacks a structured textile taxonomy aligned with industry practice, making it hard to study and benchmark methods for identifying fabric properties relevant to manipulation.
   method:  The paper proposes a textile taxonomy that jointly considers fiber material (e.g., wool, polyester, cotton) and production method (woven vs. knitted). Textile classes are determined from microscope imagery of samples to capture construction details invisible to normal cameras. A dataset of 40 labeled samples is built according to this taxonomy, and analyses (t-SNE, SVM, CNN) show construction-based labeling yields clearer separability and substantially higher accuracy than material-only labels.

13. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Learning accurate spatialization from visuals is harder with multiple concurrent sources; models need stronger audio-visual disambiguation without relying on real binaural recordings.
   method:  Augment training with an auxiliary audio-visual source separation task using only mono data. Two sources are placed at opposite image edges in the pseudo visual input, and an APNet separation branch predicts individual STFTs (Sa, Sb) under a Mix-and-Separate setup while sharing the audio backbone with the stereo task. Joint training improves the model’s ability to associate visual locations with source-specific audio, boosting binaural generation performance.

14. [CV] Learning Predictive Models From Observation and Interaction (1912.12773)
   problem: Large domain shift between observation and interaction datasets (e.g., human vs. robot embodiments, different environments) prevents naively sharing action latents across domains.
   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)
   problem: LLM-generated research ideas often lack rigorous motivational grounding in the literature due to probabilistic internal knowledge and limited context, leading to superficial or infeasible proposals.
   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).

Round 1/10.

Last search results:
(empty on first round — issue your first search)

Either issue a Search (Thought + Query) or Finalize (Reasoning + Prior works + Score).

messages.content:
You are an ambitious AI researcher scoring NOVELTY of a proposed
method through iterative literature search. Be a HARSH critic — if a
search surfaces published work using the same combination on the same
problem, novelty is low.

## Definition
Novelty = the method introduces a non-trivial combination, mechanism,
or framing that has not been published in this exact form for this
problem. Surface tweaks (renamed hyperparameter, swapped backbone of
equivalent class) and well-established combinations DO NOT qualify
as novel.

## Prior-work signal
Published literature — verified via Semantic Scholar search.
Score the method against literature precedent only; the inspired
seeds shown later are context, NOT a separate scoring axis.

## Score anchors (1-5 integer)

Anchors are written so each level can be supported by what is in the
method text itself plus literature search; do NOT reward "qualitative
trade-off" claims that require running the experiment to verify.

  1 — Identical to a published technique applied to the same problem;
      a search surfaces a paper that matches the method end-to-end.
  2 — Surface variant of a published technique (renamed hyperparameter,
      swapped backbone of an equivalent class, alternate threshold);
      a search surfaces the parent technique on the same problem.
  3 — Combines 2-3 known mechanisms with prior published joint
      application to this problem family. A search surfaces at least
      one paper that pairs the same mechanisms for a closely related
      problem.
  4 — Combines mechanism families with NO surfaced published joint
      application for this problem, OR introduces one named design
      choice (a specific new component or training signal) that is
      not in the surfaced literature. Searches return prior work on
      the individual mechanisms but not their combination as
      proposed.
  5 — Introduces a new mechanism (a named component or training
      signal not present as such in any search result), AND searches
      return no DIRECT near-clone (no paper combining the same
      mechanism families for the same problem in the same
      direction). Related work that pairs ONE of the mechanisms
      with a different counterpart, or uses the same mechanisms for
      a different problem, does NOT block 5; the bar is the absence
      of an end-to-end match.

## Reference examples — 3 query domains

### Block A — "VLM visual token compression"
  Score 5: query-conditional token-merging policy via cross-attention
           scoring tokens in one pass.
  Score 4: static attention pruning + KV-cache eviction policies
           from LLM efficiency — cross-modality non-obvious pairing.
  Score 3: established attention-magnitude pruning applied to ViT
           tokens.
  Score 2: token pruning with a different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of known pruning method.

### Block B — "compositional reasoning with discrete latent structure"
  Score 5: discrete latent composition graphs induced via learned
           merge policy + RL reward shaped by downstream task.
  Score 4: VQ-VAE codes as discrete symbols + neural composition.
  Score 3: ST Gumbel-Softmax for discrete tree induction.
  Score 2: VQ-VAE with renamed quantization function.
  Score 1: standard transformer over text.

### Block C — "time-series anomaly detection in irregular sequences"
  Score 5: time-aware transformer with continuous-time positional
           encoding + downstream-cost-tuned anomaly threshold via
           RL on alarm precision/recall trade-off.
  Score 4: Neural CDE encoder + density-ratio anomaly score —
           combination not surfaced as joint application.
  Score 3: ODE-RNN encoder with reconstruction-error scoring —
           established pattern.
  Score 2: GRU autoencoder on resampled regular intervals with a
           different threshold scheme.
  Score 1: pure restatement of an LSTM forecasting + z-score
           baseline.

## ReAct loop — output format each round (no markdown)

Each round, output EXACTLY ONE of these two formats:

Search format (issue when more evidence is needed):
    Thought: <1-3 sentences on which mechanism pairing you want to
              verify in prior work>
    Query: <semantic scholar search keywords>

Finalize format (issue ONLY when ready to score):
    Reasoning: <2-4 sentences synthesizing what searches found and
                how it maps to a score anchor>
    Prior works: <newline-separated list of papers/methods that most
                  directly cover the proposed method, one per line
                  in the form "paper_title or method_name (year): one-line
                  summary of overlap with the proposed method".
                  Use "(none)" if score is 5 with no near-clone surfaced.>
    Score: <integer 1-5>

Hard rules:
  - DO NOT mix the search and finalize formats. If you output a
    `Query:` line, the caller treats this round as a search and
    ignores any `Score:` you write — do not waste tokens on Score
    in a search round.
  - You MUST issue at least one search before finalizing UNLESS the
    method is a trivial restatement of a known technique.
  - When you write the literal token "Score:" inside Reasoning to
    cite an anchor, write it INLINE (e.g., "matches the Score 4
    anchor"), never on its own line — the parser anchors to lines
    starting with "Score:".
  - Prior works lines must each start with "  - " (two spaces, dash,
    space) so the parser can extract them. Do NOT include a trailing
    "Score:" inside a Prior works line.

## Static context (does not change across rounds)

### Research question
Aligned text-image pairs are scarce in some domains while
unimodal data is abundant; how can we learn cross-modal
representations via contrastive objectives that exploit the
abundant unimodal data?


### Proposed method
We propose UniX-Align, a semi-supervised contrastive framework that learns cross-modal image–text representations by leveraging abundant unimodal corpora and a small set of aligned pairs. Two modality-specific encoders are first pretrained with strong unimodal self-supervision (e.g., image contrastive learning and sentence-level contrastive learning) and then connected via a shared semantic prototype bank. Each encoder projects samples to a common embedding and outputs a soft assignment over K shared prototypes; the modality-specific projection heads remain distinct, while the prototype bank is shared to anchor a joint semantic space. A lightweight confidence head attached to the prototype layer provides per-sample uncertainty used to weight noisy supervision signals.

To turn unimodal data into useful cross-modal supervision, we mine pseudo pairs with two complementary channels. First, we build a cross-modal pivot space using visual taggers for images and keyword/entity extraction for text, and connect samples with high tag-overlap; a general-domain teacher (e.g., CLIP) provides soft similarity scores as additional weak signals, but these are down-weighted by the confidence head and an uncertainty-guided EMA schedule so that only stable pseudo links accumulate influence over time. Second, we construct set-level training tuples by sampling small groups of images and texts that share coarse tags. We then apply a permutation-invariant, set-to-set contrastive objective that encourages correct within-set assignments to score higher than mismatched ones, together with an auxiliary “mix-and-separate” prediction that asks the model to recover which text in the set is most responsible for each image (and vice versa). This disambiguates associations without requiring exact instance-level labels upfront.

Training follows a coarse-to-fine curriculum. We begin by aligning prototype distributions at the cluster level: separately cluster unimodal embeddings, link clusters via tag overlap and teacher scores, and minimize cross-modal divergence between their prototype assignments while preserving high-entropy, diverse usage of prototypes. As pseudo pairs stabilize, we transition to instance-level contrastive training on high-confidence matches, interleaving: (a) supervised InfoNCE on the scarce true pairs, (b) confidence-weighted InfoNCE on pseudo pairs, (c) the set-to-set mix-and-separate loss, and (d) a structure-preserving term that encourages local neighborhood consistency across modalities so that unimodal proximity is reflected cross-modally. A memory bank maintains embeddings and tag metadata to refresh pseudo neighbors periodically; pseudo-pair weights are updated with an uncertainty-aware EMA so noisy links decay while consistent links grow.

At convergence, both encoders and the shared prototype bank define an aligned space suitable for retrieval and transfer. Inference uses standard cosine similarity between image and text embeddings, with optional prototype-temperature calibration learned during training to improve zero-shot retrieval in the low-pair regime.

### Rationale
The core challenge is to exploit plentiful unimodal data without reliable alignment. UniX-Align converts unimodal corpora into usable cross-modal signals in two ways: (1) prototype-anchored alignment that ties both modalities to a common semantic scaffold learned at scale from unimodal pretraining, and (2) pseudo supervision that is robust to noise through set-level mix-and-separate objectives, confidence weighting, and an uncertainty-guided EMA curriculum. This lets the model learn which image features go with which textual concepts even when exact pairings are sparse.

Set-to-set training leverages weak co-occurrence and topical similarity to create contrastive pressure without requiring instance-level pairs, while the confidence-weighted EMA and prototype-based uncertainty mitigate confirmation bias from imperfect teachers and noisy taggers. The curriculum from cluster-level to instance-level alignment further reduces brittleness: the model first learns broad cross-modal semantics before committing to fine-grained matches, improving stability and final retrieval performance under scarce paired supervision.

### Inspired seeds (the ideation's input retrieval)
1. [IR_REC] GAG: Global Attributed Graph Neural Network for Streaming Session-based Recommendation (2007.02747)
   problem: Streaming session-based recommendation must capture short-term transitions in the current session while preserving users’ long-term preferences, but most session-based models are static, ignore user identity, or fail to adapt online.
   method:  Convert each session into a weighted directed session graph augmented with the user embedding as a global attribute, and introduce a Global Attributed Graph (GAG) neural network. GAG performs bi-directional per-edge computations conditioned on sender/receiver node features, edge weights, and the global attribute, aggregates normalized in/out neighbors to update node states, and then updates the global attribute via self-attention over item nodes conditioned on the last-clicked item and the current global attribute, with a residual connection to the user embedding. The updated global attribute becomes the session representation used to score all items. Ablations show that injecting the global attribute into both node updates and the global readout improves recommendation performance.

2. [IR_REC] Pre-training vs. Fine-tuning: A Reproducibility Study on Dense Retrieval Knowledge Acquisition (2505.07166)
   problem: Do dense retrievers acquire new retrieval knowledge during fine-tuning, or is effectiveness primarily governed by pre-trained backbones? Do prior DPR/BERT findings generalize across datasets, pooling strategies, and decoder backbones?
   method:  Conduct layer-wise linear probing by precomputing embeddings with model-specific pooling (CLS for DPR/BERT, mean for Contriever/BERT-Mean, EOS for RepLlama/Llama), then training a multiclass linear probe on concatenated query + N passage embeddings to select the relevant passage. Evaluate across Natural Questions and MS MARCO, varying N∈{2,3,4,5}, and compare fine-tuned retrievers vs their untrained backbones. Findings: For DPR (CLS), BERT-CLS often matches or outperforms DPR variants and accuracy drops as N increases, indicating pretrained knowledge underpins performance (Sec. 4.3–4.4). With mean pooling (Contriever vs BERT-Mean), the gap narrows substantially (Sec. 4.5). With a decoder backbone (RepLlama vs Llama, EOS), fine-tuning yields large late-layer gains (≈18–22% in deeper layers) (Sec. 4.6, 7), showing the DPR claim does not universally hold.

3. [LLM_NLP] Pride and Prejudice: LLM Amplifies Self-Bias in Self-Refinement (2402.11436)
   problem: LLM self-refine and self-rewarding pipelines can optimize toward falsely perceived improvements due to self-evaluation bias, causing quality to stagnate or degrade.
   method:  Empirically compare LLM self-evaluations against reference-based BLEURT and human MQM across six LLMs, four languages, and three tasks, showing universal self-bias that amplifies over iterations; in self-rewarding, increasing the candidate pool size further amplifies bias. Analyze what changes after self-refine with UniEval and style-paraphrasing experiments, finding perceived gains are mainly in fluency/understandability and style alignment rather than actual task objectives, explaining the divergence between self-estimated and true performance.

4. [CV] KOALA: A Kalman Optimization Algorithm with Loss Adaptivity (2107.03331)
   problem: Momentum improves optimization but incorporating its dynamics into a Kalman-style optimizer must remain computationally feasible for high-dimensional neural networks.
   method:  Augment the Kalman state with velocities p_k and define momentum-like dynamics x_k = x_{k-1} + p_{k-1} + w_k and p_k = κ p_{k-1} + u_k, while keeping the scalar-loss measurement h_k(x)=ˆL_k(x). Reformulate as a 2n-dimensional EKF and approximate the posterior covariance with a 2×2 block structure times identity, and approximate H_k^T H_k accordingly, yielding KOALA-M that captures momentum while retaining linear complexity in parameters. Adaptivity is further improved by online estimation of the measurement and state noise via exponential moving averages (e.g., β_R = 0.9, β_x = 0.9), reducing hyperparameter tuning and adjusting to minibatch-induced noise.

5. [ROBOTICS] Think Global, Act Local: Dual-scale Graph Transformer for Vision-and-Language Navigation (2202.11742)
   problem: Behavior cloning for sequential policies suffers from train-test distribution shift, while reinforcement learning in VLN is difficult due to sparse rewards.
   method:  DUET is first pretrained with behavior cloning and auxiliary cross-modal tasks (masked language modeling, masked region classification, single-step action prediction, and object grounding) to initialize grounded representations. It is then fine-tuned with a pseudo interactive demonstrator (PID) akin to DAgger: during rollouts, a demonstrator with access to the full environment graph selects pseudo-target actions as navigable nodes minimizing the combined distance from the current node to the node and from the node to the goal. The policy is optimized with a balance between expert and pseudo labels, yielding stronger navigation performance than RL-based fine-tuning in experiments.

6. [ROBOTICS] Multiscale Sensor Fusion and Continuous Control with Neural CDEs (2203.08715)
   problem: Continuous-time CDE policies must be causal for real-time deployment, but natural cubic spline interpolation induces non-causal dependencies on future data.
   method:  Propose a discretely-online CDE evolution: output control at a fixed high rate and evolve the CDE over successive short intervals, where at each step the cubic spline is fit only to measurements within the current interval. This preserves causality at the control frequency while retaining smooth integration of high-rate signals between sparse images. For truly continuous-time control, a zero-order-hold interpolant can be used. This strategy enables practical, real-time deployment of CDE-driven policies without violating causality.

7. [IR_REC] Efficient Pointwise-Pairwise Learning-to-Rank for News Recommendation (2409.17711)
   problem: Pairwise and listwise inference can significantly improve ranking quality but are computationally prohibitive at inference time for large candidate sets.
   method:  GLIMPSE introduces an efficient rank aggregation strategy that initializes with a pointwise-ranked list and performs right-to-left (RTL) passes that only compare adjacent items among the top-k results, swapping when the pairwise model prefers the right item. Each RTL pass over k items requires just k−1 comparisons, enabling O(K) complexity per pass versus O(K^2) for all-pairs approaches. Hyperparameters control the number of RTL passes (m) and the top-k scope, delivering practical gains with very few pairwise calls while preserving scalability.

The sensor provides 0.01 kPa resolution over 50–1500 kPa, detects forces as low as 0.007 N and as high as 212 N, and exhibits a linear sensitivity of ~7.24 kPa/N. A radial basis function interpolation reconstructs a continuous spatial pressure map from the 8 taxels, enabling coarse shape discrimination despite the sparse array. (Supported: lines 60–75, 62–63, 138–140).

   method:  Partition the latent z into shared and domain-specific components (z_shared, z_domain) and learn domain-dependent priors. Use a shared Gaussian prior for z_shared across domains, and separate learned Gaussian priors for z_domain in each domain; for human observation data, parameterize the z_domain prior temporally with an LSTM conditioned on the initial frame to capture correlated human actions. This domain-dependent prior enables leveraging human and dashboard videos to improve robot/driving action-conditioned prediction and downstream planning.

15. [MULTIMODAL] Visually Informed Binaural Audio Generation without Binaural Audios (2104.06162)
   problem: Supervised stereo models often overfit to datasets and existing evaluation metrics underweight phase errors that determine perceived direction.
   method:  Use pseudo visual-stereo pairs as data augmentation (Augment-PseudoBinaural) alongside real binaural recordings to improve supervised training, yielding consistent gains across FAIR-Play and MUSIC-Stereo. Additionally, propose Difference Phase Distance (D_phase), a phase-sensitive metric computed on the difference spectrum between left and right channels (comparing the phase of SD and the predicted M*Sm), making evaluation more aligned with directional perception.

16. [LLM_NLP] BioDEX: Large-Scale Biomedical Adverse Drug Event Extraction for Real-World Pharmacovigilance (2305.13395)
   problem: PV literature triage often relies on naive keyword lookups for reactions/drugs, yielding low precision when identifying papers that truly report ADEs for targets of interest.
   method:  The authors repurpose model-extracted reactions/drugs to perform per-paper binary classification (e.g., whether a reaction is part of an ADE), demonstrating higher macro-F1 than lookup baselines across the 30 most frequent reactions (and similarly for drugs). This suggests the extractor can prioritize relevant papers more effectively in real PV workflows.

17. [CV] Hit-Detector: Hierarchical Trinity Architecture Search for Object Detection (2003.11818)
   problem: Directly searching a very large, shared operation space across all detector components is computationally prohibitive and inefficient because different parts (backbone, neck, head) prefer different operators.
   method:  Propose hierarchical screening to automatically derive tailored sub search spaces per component using column-sparse (group sparsity) regularization over the architecture-parameter matrix to rank and prune operation candidates. Sequentially filter from a 32-op global space down to compact per-part subspaces (e.g., Nb=Nn=Nh=8), then run the end-to-end differentiable search within these tailored spaces. Ablations show that using three specialized subspaces improves mAP from 40.1% (single shared subspace) to 41.4%, and the sparsity term (µ≈0.1) stabilizes and sharpens operation selection.

18. [ROBOTICS] Learning to Scaffold the Development of Robotic Manipulation Skills (1911.00969)
   problem: Skills learned with fixtures often fail once the fixtures are removed; naive removal causes large performance drops.
   method:  Replace hard fixture constraints by a soft, repulsive potential field that matches the fixture geometry inferred from depth, and gradually move the physical fixture away while continuing to train the inner-loop policy initialized from the fixture-trained policy. On peg insertion, this curriculum recovers performance to about 70% success when the fixture is fully removed, versus around 10% with naive removal, maintaining the task skill without physical scaffolding.

19. [MULTIMODAL] VideoEspresso: A Large-Scale Chain-of-Thought Dataset for Fine-Grained Video Reasoning via Core Frame Selection (2411.14794)
   problem: Reasoning over videos with LVLMs is computationally expensive due to many redundant frames and long contexts, and models often lack structured, evidence-driven reasoning.
   method:  Propose Hybrid LVLMs Collaboration for VideoQA: a lightweight Frame Selector followed by a fine-grained reasoning LVLM. A tiny LVLM (~1B) captions sampled frames and a tiny LLM (~0.5–1.5B) selects question-relevant core frames; the reasoning LVLM is trained with a two-stage SFT—stage-1 generates multimodal evidence and stage-2 answers using that evidence. At inference, the selector reduces inputs to around 2–3 core frames, then the LVLM performs CoT-based reasoning, yielding higher accuracy and substantially lower TFLOPs on a 14-task benchmark; the selector is plug-and-play and improves efficiency across different LVLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, InternVL2).

20. [CV] Latent Discriminant Deterministic Uncertainty (2207.10130)
   problem: Deterministic uncertainty methods often rely on bi-Lipschitz constraints that hinder scalability and stability on complex, high-resolution vision tasks (e.g., semantic segmentation), while ensemble/sampling methods are computationally expensive.
   method:  Introduce LDU, which inserts a distinction maximization (DM) layer with an arbitrary number of trainable prototypes into the pre-logit latent space and measures prototype similarity via cosine similarity, followed by an exponential activation to sharpen responses. This learns a discriminative latent space without enforcing Lipschitz constraints, enabling single forward-pass uncertainty estimation that scales to classification, semantic segmentation, and depth regression. An auxiliary MLP head g_unc operates on the DM embedding to output a confidence score. Experiments show competitive uncertainty performance with Deep Ensembles across tasks at much lower computational cost.

21. [LLM_NLP] MotivGraph-SoIQ: Integrating Motivational Knowledge Graphs and Socratic Dialogue for Enhanced LLM Ideation (2509.21978)

   method:  Introduce MotivGraph, a motivational knowledge graph that encodes research motivations as (problem, challenge, solution) triples with explicit edge types (parent-of, problem–challenge, challenge–solution). Build it automatically via SciMotivMiner, which extracts standardized, author-agnostic triples from papers and merges duplicates, then add a hierarchy using a Parent Node Addition algorithm that embeds nodes, finds nearest neighbors, and leverages an LLM to form general parent nodes. During ideation, agents use graph tools (fuzzy node search and relation retrieval) to ground ideas on concrete nodes from the graph, supplying structured, cross-linked motivational context for each proposal.

## Tie-breaking
When between two adjacent scores, default to the LOWER. Resist
optimism bias toward "novel". Score against the literature
signal only; do not apply an additional penalty when the method
reuses inspired seeds (the seeds are context, not a benchmark).
